messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

Honestly it's one of those things where the answer is kind of unsatisfying - hiccups don't really seem to have a useful purpose *now*, but there are some interesting theories about why we have the reflex at all.

The basic mechanics are pretty clear - your diaphragm spasms, you inhale suddenly, and then your vocal cords snap shut, making that characteristic sound. But the *why* is more debated.

One theory that gets thrown around a lot is that it's an evolutionary leftover from when our ancestors were aquatic - like a reflex that helped ancient gill-breathing creatures clear water from their gills. The hiccup reflex is surprisingly similar to how some fish and amphibians move water over their gills. It's a fun idea but honestly pretty speculative.

Another angle is that it might have helped infant mammals nurse - the reflex could help expel air from the stomach while feeding, kind of like a built-in burping mechanism. That one actually has some decent support.

But the honest answer is that for adult humans, hiccups seem pretty pointless and are mostly just annoying. They're one of those things biology kept around even though it doesn't serve much obvious function anymore.

What's funny is we have all these folk remedies for stopping them - holding your breath, drinking water upside down - and none of them are definitively proven to work consistently. Which kind of tells you how little we really understand them.

Did you have a random hiccup moment that got you thinking about this? 😄
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

Aw poor kid, long hiccup sessions are the worst! There's something especially pitiful about watching a little one just getting hit with them over and over and not being able to do anything about it 😄

How old is she? Because babies and young kids seem to get them SO much more frequently than adults. Newborns especially can hiccup almost constantly and it looks so dramatic on such a tiny person.

The good news is that for kids they're almost always completely harmless even when they go on for a while. It usually just takes time.

The threshold where doctors start paying attention is something like hiccups lasting more than 48 hours straight in adults, which sounds wild but apparently can happen and can sometimes signal something worth checking out. But a long session for a kid is pretty normal.

Did she eventually get rid of them on their own or did you guys try any remedies? I'm always curious what people swear by because everyone seems to have their family's "guaranteed" cure 😄
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The sugar one is actually one of the more plausible folk remedies honestly! The idea is that swallowing something with a strong texture or taste kind of "resets" or overwhelms the vagus nerve which is involved in the hiccup reflex. So there's at least a logical mechanism behind it even if it's not proven.

Mary Poppins would approve too 😄

Some of the ones people swear by are genuinely hilarious though - like getting scared, drinking water from the far side of the glass (which just seems like a great way to spill water on yourself), or pulling on your tongue. There's something so funny about the image of someone desperately trying all these weird tricks while hiccuping the whole time.

The holding your breath one probably has the most going for it scientifically - the idea is that building up CO2 in your system can calm the diaphragm down. Which is also why breathing into a paper bag gets suggested.

But honestly the fact that we have like 50 different "cures" and none of them work reliably for everyone kind of proves that sometimes they just stop on their own and we give the remedy credit 😄

Did your grandma's sugar trick seem to actually work or was it more of a comfort/distraction thing for you as a kid?
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

Right?? It's one of those things that makes you realize how much we just take for granted that science has figured everything out, and then you pull on a thread like "why do hiccups exist" and it's like...actually we're not totally sure 😄

And hiccups aren't even alone in that category - there are a bunch of super common everyday things that are surprisingly poorly understood. Like yawning is another one, we still don't fully know why we do it or why it's contagious. Same with why we dream, or why we need sleep at the level we do.

It's kind of humbling in a good way I think. Like the human body is this thing we've lived in our entire lives and medicine has made incredible advances, but there are still these basic quirky things happening in us that are genuinely mysterious.

There's something almost charming about it - your niece sitting there hiccuping away and she's basically demonstrating an unsolved biological puzzle 😄

It also makes you appreciate that a lot of what gets passed down as folk wisdom like your grandma's sugar trick comes from people just genuinely trying to figure things out before there were scientists to ask. That's kind of sweet when you think about it.

Does your family have any other weird folk remedies or is the sugar thing the main one you remember?
